23 July 1581 by [Mathieu Moullart](/w/index.php?title=Mathieu_Moullart&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mathieu Moullart (page does not exist)"), [bishop of Arras](/wiki/Bishop_of_Arras "Bishop of Arras")
**[Bishop of Saint-Omer](/wiki/Bishop_of_Saint-Omer "Bishop of Saint-Omer")**  1581–1586 | Succeeded by [Jacobus Pamelius](/wiki/Jacobus_Pamelius "Jacobus Pamelius")
(1533–1586) was a theologian and [bishop of Saint-Omer](/wiki/Ancient_Diocese_of_Saint-Omer "Ancient Diocese of Saint-Omer").

A. C. De Schrevel, "Lucas, François, dit Lucas Brugensis", *[Biographie Nationale de Belgique](/wiki/Biographie_Nationale_de_Belgique "Biographie Nationale de Belgique")*, [vol. 12](http://academieroyale.be/Academie/documents/FichierPDFBiographieNationaleTome2053.pdf) (Brussels, 1893), 550-563.

first_paragraph: **Jean Six** (1533–1586) was a theologian and [bishop of Saint-Omer](/wiki/Ancient_Diocese_of_Saint-Omer "Ancient Diocese of Saint-Omer").

**Jean Six** (1533–1586) was a theologian and [bishop of Saint-Omer](/wiki/Ancient_Diocese_of_Saint-Omer "Ancient Diocese of Saint-Omer").

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Six&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

Six was born in [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille"), [Walloon Flanders](/wiki/Walloon_Flanders "Walloon Flanders"), in 1533, the son of a city magistrate. He studied at [Leuven University](/wiki/Old_University_of_Leuven "Old University of Leuven"), graduating [Master of Arts](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts") in 1551. From 1552 to 1558 he taught Philosophy at Lily College, while studying Theology at [Holy Spirit College](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_College_(Leuven) "Holy Spirit College (Leuven)"), graduating [Licentiate in Sacred Theology](/wiki/Licentiate_in_Sacred_Theology "Licentiate in Sacred Theology"). On 21 June 1561 he succeeded [Jean Hessels](/wiki/Jean_Hessels "Jean Hessels") as head of the theological college.[[1]](#cite_note-BNB-1)

On 31 January 1563 he resigned in favour of [Cornelius Jansen](/wiki/Cornelius_Jansen_(Bishop_of_Ghent) "Cornelius Jansen (Bishop of Ghent)") to become parish priest of the [Church of Saint-Étienne, Lille](/wiki/Church_of_Saint-%C3%89tienne,_Lille "Church of Saint-Étienne, Lille"). On 3 December 1571 [Gérard de Haméricourt](/wiki/G%C3%A9rard_de_Ham%C3%A9ricourt "Gérard de Haméricourt"), bishop of Saint-Omer, appointed him to a [canonry](/wiki/Canon_(priest) "Canon (priest)") of [Saint-Omer Cathedral](/wiki/Saint-Omer_Cathedral "Saint-Omer Cathedral"). Six signed the [Union of Brussels](/wiki/Union_of_Brussels "Union of Brussels") on 9 January 1577 on behalf of the bishop. After Haméricourt's death, on 17 March 1577, Six represented the clergy in the [States](/wiki/Estates_of_the_realm "Estates of the realm") of the [County of Artois](/wiki/County_of_Artois "County of Artois"), and was deputized to represent the States of Artois in the [Estates General](/wiki/States_General_of_the_Netherlands#Burgundian_and_Habsburg_rule "States General of the Netherlands"), where he took the rotating presidency whenever it was the county's turn. His intransigence towards the Calvinists led to his recall by the States.[[1]](#cite_note-BNB-1)

On 7 October 1577 he was appointed [canon penitentiary](/wiki/Canon_penitentiary "Canon penitentiary"), and on 20 December 1577 [diocesan administrator](/wiki/Diocesan_administrator "Diocesan administrator"). When the rebels briefly took power in [Saint-Omer](/wiki/Saint-Omer "Saint-Omer"), he refused to take an oath of loyalty to [William of Orange](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent"), and on 22 April 1578 was banished from the city. During his exile he studied Hebrew at the University of Paris. He returned to Saint-Omer in February 1579, after the [Union of Arras](/wiki/Union_of_Arras "Union of Arras") had been agreed. On 17 August 1579 he was reinstated as diocesan administrator, and was appointed [archdeacon](/wiki/Archdeacon "Archdeacon") of Artois.[[1]](#cite_note-BNB-1)

On 6 May 1580, the governor general of the Habsburg Netherlands, [Alexander Farnese](/wiki/Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma "Alexander Farnese, Duke of Parma"), sent Six the royal letters naming him bishop of Saint-Omer. [Pope Gregory XIII](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_XIII "Pope Gregory XIII") confirmed the nomination on 3 March 1581. He was consecrated bishop in Douai on 23 July 1581 by [Mathieu Moulart](/wiki/Mathieu_Moulart "Mathieu Moulart"), [bishop of Arras](/wiki/Bishop_of_Arras "Bishop of Arras"), assisted by [Jean Sarazin](/wiki/Jean_Sarazin "Jean Sarazin"), [abbot of St Vaast](/wiki/Abbot_of_St_Vaast "Abbot of St Vaast"), and [Arnold Gantois](/w/index.php?title=Arnold_Gantois&action=edit&redlink=1 "Arnold Gantois (page does not exist)"), abbot of [Marchiennes Abbey](/wiki/Marchiennes_Abbey "Marchiennes Abbey"). He was installed in his see on 6 August. In December 1582 he formally adopted the [Gregorian Calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_Calendar "Gregorian Calendar") in his diocese.[[1]](#cite_note-BNB-1)

After the conclusion of the [Siege of Ypres (1584)](/w/index.php?title=Siege_of_Ypres_(1584)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Siege of Ypres (1584) (page does not exist)"), Six was invited to that city to reconsecrate the churches. In January 1585 he assisted at the double consecrations in Tournai of [Petrus Simons](/wiki/Petrus_Simons "Petrus Simons") as [bishop of Ypres](/wiki/Bishop_of_Ypres "Bishop of Ypres") and [Clemens Crabbeels](/wiki/Clemens_Crabbeels "Clemens Crabbeels") as [bishop of 's-Hertogenbosch](/wiki/Bishop_of_%27s-Hertogenbosch "Bishop of 's-Hertogenbosch").[[1]](#cite_note-BNB-1)

He died in Lille on 11 October 1586, while en route to attend a [provincial synod](/wiki/Provincial_synod "Provincial synod") in [Mons](/wiki/Mons,_Belgium "Mons, Belgium").[[1]](#cite_note-BNB-1) His secretary, [Franciscus Lucas Brugensis](/wiki/Franciscus_Lucas_Brugensis "Franciscus Lucas Brugensis"), transported his heart back to Saint-Omer for burial there.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Six&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Publications")]

* *Statuta synodi dioecesanae audomarensis, anno M D LXXXIII* (Douai, [Jan Bogard](/wiki/Jan_Bogard "Jan Bogard"), 1583), the statutes of the diocesan synod of 1583, introducing Tridentine reforms.
